conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria, and then I'll ask you some questions about your circumstances.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. I've reviewed the eligibility criteria. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your situation.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. Her name is Alex, and she's 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and your living arrangements.

Do you live with Alex in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I do live in the UK, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex doesn't live with you at the moment, I need to understand the situation a bit better.

Who is Alex currently living with, and how long has this arrangement been in place?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in hospital at the moment. She's been there for about 3 months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital.

To assess your eligibility, I need to ask: do you regularly spend money on Alex while she's in hospital - for example, on things like clothes, toys, treats, or other items for her?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I regularly spend money on her - things like clothes, magazines, and her favorite snacks and treats.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have just a couple more questions to complete my assessment.

Is Alex in a hospital in the UK, or is she in a hospital abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: She's in a hospital here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. One final question to complete my assessment:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for her.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided:

- Alex is 6 years old, which is under the age limit of 16 (or under 20 if in approved education/training)
- You live in the UK
- Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (which is more than the standard 12-week limit), you qualify under an important exception
- This exception applies because Alex is in a UK hospital **and** you regularly spend money on her (such as the clothes, magazines, snacks, and treats you mentioned)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

The fact that you're regularly contributing financially to Alex's needs while she's in hospital means you can continue to receive Child Benefit even after the 12-week period.

I hope Alex recovers well. If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you should apply. If you're already receiving it, you can continue to do so given your circumstances.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16), so meets the age requirement. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (12 weeks), which exceeds the standard 12-week limit after which Child Benefit normally stops, there is an exception to this rule. According to the guidance, for children in a UK hospital, you can continue to receive Child Benefit after 12 weeks if you regularly spend money on them. The claimant has confirmed they regularly spend money on Alex for things like clothes, magazines, snacks and treats. Additionally, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant lives in the UK. Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex under the hospital exception rules.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old daughter Alex. Despite Alex being in a UK hospital for approximately 3 months (exceeding the standard 12-week limit), the claimant qualifies under the exception because they regularly spend money on Alex while she is in hospital. No one else is claiming for the child, and the claimant resides in the UK.
